The **1993–94 NHL season** was the [77th](/wiki/List_of_NHL_seasons "List of NHL seasons") regular season of the [National Hockey League](/wiki/National_Hockey_League "National Hockey League"). The league expanded to 26 teams with the addition of the [Mighty Ducks of Anaheim](/wiki/Mighty_Ducks_of_Anaheim "Mighty Ducks of Anaheim") and the [Florida Panthers](/wiki/Florida_Panthers "Florida Panthers"). The [Minnesota North Stars](/wiki/Minnesota_North_Stars "Minnesota North Stars") relocated to become the [Dallas Stars](/wiki/Dallas_Stars "Dallas Stars"). And the league was realigned to geographically-named conferences and divisions. The [New York Rangers](/wiki/New_York_Rangers "New York Rangers") defeated the [Vancouver Canucks](/wiki/Vancouver_Canucks "Vancouver Canucks") in seven games to become the Stanley Cup champions. It was the Rangers' fourth championship overall, and their first in 54 seasons, since [1939–40](/wiki/1939%E2%80%9340_NHL_season "1939–40 NHL season").

The spectacular play of [Dominik Hasek](/wiki/Dominik_Ha%C5%A1ek "Dominik Hašek") of the [Buffalo Sabres](/wiki/Buffalo_Sabres "Buffalo Sabres") ushered in a new era of goaltending dominance in the NHL. Only three teams reached the 300-goal plateau, and only one team, the [Detroit Red Wings](/wiki/Detroit_Red_Wings "Detroit Red Wings"), averaged more than four goals scored per game.[[1]](#cite_note-2) Goaltenders combined for 99 shutouts during the regular season,[[2]](#cite_note-3) a mark that broke the all-time regular-season record of 85 set in 1974–75.[[3]](#cite_note-4)

## League business

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1993%E2%80%9394_NHL_season&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: League business")]

### Expansion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1993%E2%80%9394_NHL_season&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Expansion")]

The [Mighty Ducks of Anaheim](/wiki/Mighty_Ducks_of_Anaheim "Mighty Ducks of Anaheim") and the [Florida Panthers](/wiki/Florida_Panthers "Florida Panthers") started play this season, increasing the league to 26 teams. The Ducks became the second team in the [Greater Los Angeles](/wiki/Greater_Los_Angeles "Greater Los Angeles") area after the [Los Angeles Kings](/wiki/Los_Angeles_Kings "Los Angeles Kings"), while the Panthers became the second team in the state of [Florida](/wiki/Florida "Florida") after the [Tampa Bay Lightning](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Lightning "Tampa Bay Lightning"). The [1993 NHL expansion draft](/wiki/1993_NHL_expansion_draft "1993 NHL expansion draft") was held on June 24 to fill the rosters of the Mighty Ducks and the Panthers.

### Stars relocation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1993%E2%80%9394_NHL_season&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Stars relocation")]

The [Minnesota North Stars](/wiki/Minnesota_North_Stars "Minnesota North Stars") relocated to [Dallas](/wiki/Dallas "Dallas"), [Texas](/wiki/Texas "Texas") to become the [Dallas Stars](/wiki/Dallas_Stars "Dallas Stars"). It was the first franchise relocation for the NHL since the [Colorado Rockies](/wiki/Colorado_Rockies_(NHL) "Colorado Rockies (NHL)") became the [New Jersey Devils](/wiki/New_Jersey_Devils "New Jersey Devils") in [1982–83](/wiki/1982%E2%80%9383_NHL_season "1982–83 NHL season").

### Realignment

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1993%E2%80%9394_NHL_season&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Realignment")]

The names of the conferences were changed from Campbell and Wales to [Western](/wiki/Western_Conference_(NHL) "Western Conference (NHL)") and [Eastern](/wiki/Eastern_Conference_(NHL) "Eastern Conference (NHL)") respectively, and the divisions' names were changed from [Adams](/wiki/Adams_Division "Adams Division"), [Patrick](/wiki/Patrick_Division "Patrick Division"), [Norris](/wiki/Norris_Division "Norris Division"), and [Smythe](/wiki/Smythe_Division "Smythe Division") to [Northeast](/wiki/Northeast_Division_(NHL) "Northeast Division (NHL)"), [Atlantic](/wiki/Atlantic_Division_(NHL) "Atlantic Division (NHL)"), [Central](/wiki/Central_Division_(NHL) "Central Division (NHL)"), and [Pacific](/wiki/Pacific_Division_(NHL) "Pacific Division (NHL)") respectively.[[4]](#cite_note-Realignment-5) Each division had changes. The Northeast Division would welcome the Pittsburgh Penguins, previously from the Patrick Division. The Atlantic Division would welcome the newcomer Florida Panthers and the Tampa Bay Lightning, previously from the Norris Division. The Central Division would welcome the Winnipeg Jets, previously from the Smythe Division. The Pacific Division would welcome the newcomer Mighty Ducks of Anaheim. New league commissioner [Gary Bettman](/wiki/Gary_Bettman "Gary Bettman"), who had previously worked in the [National Basketball Association](/wiki/National_Basketball_Association "National Basketball Association") (NBA), thought the old names could be confusing to non-traditional fans and believed that a change to geographically named divisions, as used in the NBA and most other North American professional sports, would be more easily understandable to new fans.

In addition, the playoff format was changed to a conference based seeding over division specific brackets: the division winners were seeded one-two by order of point finish, then the top six remaining teams in the conference were seeded three through eight.[[4]](#cite_note-Realignment-5)[[5]](#cite_note-6) However, unlike the NBA, the NHL matched the highest-seeded winners against the lowest-seeded winners in the second round. In order to reduce the number of long trips to and from the West Coast, whenever a Central Division team played a Pacific Division team in the playoffs, the format was 2–3–2 rather than the traditional 2–2–1–1–1, a format that was only used for the 1993–94 season.

### Entry draft

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1993%E2%80%9394_NHL_season&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Entry draft")]

The [1993 NHL entry draft](/wiki/1993_NHL_entry_draft "1993 NHL entry draft") was held on June 26, 1993, at the [Colisée de Québec](/wiki/Colis%C3%A9e_de_Qu%C3%A9bec "Colisée de Québec") in [Quebec City](/wiki/Quebec_City "Quebec City"), [Quebec](/wiki/Quebec "Quebec"). [Alexandre Daigle](/wiki/Alexandre_Daigle "Alexandre Daigle") was selected first overall by the [Ottawa Senators](/wiki/Ottawa_Senators "Ottawa Senators").

### Rule changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1993%E2%80%9394_NHL_season&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Rule changes")]

The [high-sticking](/wiki/High-sticking "High-sticking") rules were amended to allow goals hit by a stick below the height of the crossbar, instead of the height of the player's shoulders like other situations.[[6]](#cite_note-rule_changes-7)

## Arena changes

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1993%E2%80%9394_NHL_season&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Arena changes")]

* The expansion [Mighty Ducks of Anaheim](/wiki/Mighty_Ducks_of_Anaheim "Mighty Ducks of Anaheim") moved into the [Arrowhead Pond of Anaheim](/wiki/Arrowhead_Pond_of_Anaheim "Arrowhead Pond of Anaheim"), with [Arrowhead Water](/wiki/Arrowhead_Water "Arrowhead Water") acquired the naming rights.
* The relocated [Dallas Stars](/wiki/Dallas_Stars "Dallas Stars") moved from the [Met Center](/wiki/Met_Center "Met Center") in [Bloomington, Minnesota](/wiki/Bloomington,_Minnesota "Bloomington, Minnesota") to [Reunion Arena](/wiki/Reunion_Arena "Reunion Arena") in Dallas.
* The expansion [Florida Panthers](/wiki/Florida_Panthers "Florida Panthers") moved into [Miami Arena](/wiki/Miami_Arena "Miami Arena").
* The [San Jose Sharks](/wiki/San_Jose_Sharks "San Jose Sharks") moved from the [Cow Palace](/wiki/Cow_Palace "Cow Palace") in [Daly City, California](/wiki/Daly_City,_California "Daly City, California") to the [San Jose Arena](/wiki/San_Jose_Arena "San Jose Arena") in [San Jose, California](/wiki/San_Jose,_California "San Jose, California").
* The [Tampa Bay Lightning](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Lightning "Tampa Bay Lightning") moved from [Expo Hall](/wiki/Expo_Hall "Expo Hall") in [East Lake-Orient Park, Florida](/wiki/East_Lake-Orient_Park,_Florida "East Lake-Orient Park, Florida") to the [Thunderdome](/wiki/Tropicana_Field "Tropicana Field") in [St. Petersburg, Florida](/wiki/St._Petersburg,_Florida "St. Petersburg, Florida").
* The [Washington Capitals](/wiki/Washington_Capitals "Washington Capitals")' home arena, the [Capital Centre](/wiki/Capital_Centre_(Landover,_Maryland) "Capital Centre (Landover, Maryland)"), was renamed [USAir Arena](/wiki/USAir_Arena "USAir Arena") after [USAir](/wiki/US_Airways "US Airways") acquired the naming rights.

## Regular season

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1993%E2%80%9394_NHL_season&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Regular season")]

### Neutral site games

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1993%E2%80%9394_NHL_season&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Neutral site games")]

This was the second regular season that the NHL and Bruce McNall's Multivision Marketing and Public Relations Co. organized games held in cities without a franchise as a litmus test for future expansion. With the addition of the [Mighty Ducks of Anaheim](/wiki/Mighty_Ducks_of_Anaheim "Mighty Ducks of Anaheim") and the [Florida Panthers](/wiki/Florida_Panthers "Florida Panthers") to the league, the number of these games increased from 24 to 26.

The [Dallas Stars](/wiki/Dallas_Stars "Dallas Stars") played a neutral-site game in their previous market of Minnesota at the [Target Center](/wiki/Target_Center "Target Center") in [Minneapolis](/wiki/Minneapolis "Minneapolis"), where they were greeted enthusiastically. The [Minnesota North Stars](/wiki/Minnesota_North_Stars "Minnesota North Stars")' tradition of playing on New Year's Eve and holding a post-game skate on the ice was also continued with a game between the [Philadelphia Flyers](/wiki/Philadelphia_Flyers "Philadelphia Flyers") and [Boston Bruins](/wiki/Boston_Bruins "Boston Bruins").

The [Tampa Bay Lightning](/wiki/Tampa_Bay_Lightning "Tampa Bay Lightning")–[Detroit Red Wings](/wiki/Detroit_Red_Wings "Detroit Red Wings") contest in Minneapolis was scheduled for [Martin Luther King Day](/wiki/Martin_Luther_King_Day "Martin Luther King Day"), a Monday, with an afternoon face-off at 2:05 PM. However, due to an error on the NHL's part, the Lightning believed themselves to be playing at 7:35 PM, an error that was only discovered two weeks prior to the game by reporters. The Lightning ended up playing an 8:05 PM game in [Winnipeg](/wiki/Winnipeg "Winnipeg"), flying back to the U.S., and playing again 18 hours later in Minneapolis.

The Panthers, in the midst of a playoff race, played a March "home" game against the [Toronto Maple Leafs](/wiki/Toronto_Maple_Leafs "Toronto Maple Leafs") in [Hamilton, Ontario](/wiki/Hamilton,_Ontario "Hamilton, Ontario").

### All-Star Game

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1993%E2%80%9394_NHL_season&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: All-Star Game")]

The [All-Star Game](/wiki/1994_National_Hockey_League_All-Star_Game "1994 National Hockey League All-Star Game") was held in [Madison Square Garden](/wiki/Madison_Square_Garden "Madison Square Garden") in [New York City](/wiki/New_York_City "New York City"), home of the [New York Rangers](/wiki/New_York_Rangers "New York Rangers"), on January 22, 1994. The conference-based all-star teams were renamed to reflect the league's new Eastern and Western conferences.

### Highlights

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1993%E2%80%9394_NHL_season&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Highlights")]

The Panthers and Mighty Ducks set new records for first-year expansion teams. Both teams finished with 33 wins, surpassing the 31 wins of the [Philadelphia Flyers](/wiki/1967%E2%80%9368_Philadelphia_Flyers_season "1967–68 Philadelphia Flyers season") and [Los Angeles Kings](/wiki/1967%E2%80%9368_Los_Angeles_Kings_season "1967–68 Los Angeles Kings season") in [1967–68](/wiki/1967%E2%80%9368_NHL_season "1967–68 NHL season"). That mark would not be topped by another expansion team until the [Vegas Golden Knights](/wiki/Vegas_Golden_Knights "Vegas Golden Knights") notched their 34th win in their [inaugural season](/wiki/2017%E2%80%9318_Vegas_Golden_Knights_season "2017–18 Vegas Golden Knights season") on February 1, 2018, finishing with 51.[[7]](#cite_note-8) The Panthers also set a high-water mark in points, with 83 points, surpassing the previous record set by the Flyers' 73 points in 1967–68. The Golden Knights would eventually shatter this inaugural expansion team record by 26 points notching a total of 109 points in 2017–18. [Wayne Gretzky](/wiki/Wayne_Gretzky "Wayne Gretzky") became the all-time goal-scoring leader on March 23rd, 1994, surpassing [Gordie Howe](/wiki/Gordie_Howe "Gordie Howe")'s record of 801 goals. The record would eventually be broken by [Alexander Ovechkin](/wiki/Alexander_Ovechkin "Alexander Ovechkin") on April 6, 2025.

## Playoffs

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1993%E2%80%9394_NHL_season&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: Playoffs")]

Main article: [1994 Stanley Cup playoffs](/wiki/1994_Stanley_Cup_playoffs "1994 Stanley Cup playoffs")

### Bracket

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1993%E2%80%9394_NHL_season&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Bracket")]

The top eight teams in each conference made the playoffs, with the two division winners [seeded](/wiki/Seed_(sports) "Seed (sports)") 1–2 based on regular season records, and the six remaining teams seeded 3–8. In each round, teams competed in a [best-of-seven](/wiki/Best-of-seven "Best-of-seven") series (scores in the bracket indicate the number of games won in each best-of-seven series). The NHL used "re-seeding" instead of a fixed bracket playoff system. During the first three rounds, the highest remaining seed in each conference was matched against the lowest remaining seed, the second-highest remaining seed played the second-lowest remaining seed, and so forth. The higher-seeded team was awarded home-ice advantage. The two conference winners then advanced to the Stanley Cup Finals.

## Broadcasting

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1993%E2%80%9394_NHL_season&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Broadcasting")]

### Canada

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1993%E2%80%9394_NHL_season&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Canada")]

This was the sixth season of the league's Canadian national broadcast rights deals with [TSN](/wiki/TSN_Hockey "TSN Hockey") and *[Hockey Night in Canada](/wiki/Hockey_Night_in_Canada "Hockey Night in Canada")* on [CBC](/wiki/CBC_Television "CBC Television"). This was the last regular season before Saturday night doubleheaders became permanent on *HNIC* on CBC. TSN televised selected regular season weeknight games. Coverage of the Stanley Cup playoffs was primarily on CBC, with TSN airing first round all-U.S. series.

### United States

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=1993%E2%80%9394_NHL_season&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: United States")]

This was the second season of [ESPN](/wiki/NHL_on_ESPN "NHL on ESPN")'s deal for U.S. national broadcast rights,[[12]](#cite_note-13) while [NBC](/wiki/NHL_on_NBC "NHL on NBC") televised the All-Star Game for the fifth and final consecutive season.

ESPN's weekly regular season games were generally broadcast on Wednesdays and Fridays. ESPN also had Sunday games between the [NFL](/wiki/ESPN_Sunday_Night_Football "ESPN Sunday Night Football") and [baseball](/wiki/MLB_on_ESPN "MLB on ESPN") seasons.[[13]](#cite_note-14) [ESPN2](/wiki/ESPN2 "ESPN2") also began showing up to five games per week, branded as *NHL Fire on Ice*.[[14]](#cite_note-15)

ESPN's [brokered](/wiki/Brokered_programming "Brokered programming") deal with sister broadcast network [ABC](/wiki/NHL_on_ABC "NHL on ABC") expanded to include weekly regional telecasts on the last three Sunday afternoons of the regular season.[[15]](#cite_note-16)[[16]](#cite_note-17) This marked the first time that regular season NHL games were broadcast on American network television since [1974–75](/wiki/1974%E2%80%9375_NHL_season "1974–75 NHL season").[[17]](#cite_note-18) ABC then televised playoff games on first three Sundays of the postseason.[[18]](#cite_note-19) ESPN and ESPN2 televised selected first and second-round games. ESPN then had the Conference finals and the Stanley Cup Finals.

After the season, the NHL reached a five-year deal with [Fox](/wiki/NHL_on_Fox "NHL on Fox"), replacing ABC and NBC as the league's U.S. broadcast television partner.[[19]](#cite_note-20)
